[Question]
How do the biases (aj and bi) influence the energy function and consequently affect the training process and performance of the Restricted Boltzmann Machine described in this section?

The biases aj and bi are constant values used to shift the energy function during the training process. By adjusting these biases, the energy function is shifted to improve the model's performance. Specifically, aj influences the visible units vj, and bi influences the hidden units hi, thereby affecting the probabilities and the overall energy landscape that the Restricted Boltzmann Machine learns.

C. Label Generation
After all features are generated, we need to assign targets or
“labels” for our machine learning models. The labels are
obtained by mining analyst’s investigation notes, including but
not limited to:

Initial background: the reasons why an alert was
triggered.

Internal research: support information from different
internal system logs.

External research: support information from external
resources, such as IP geolocation and reputation.

Investigation result: whether the event is non-
malicious, false positive, or escalated.
Text mining techniques, such as key word/topic extraction
and sentiment analysis, are required to extract the host’s actual


Topic 1: “Unable to gather supporting evidence for



this host”   Host status from text mining: risky

[Question]
How does the process of mining analyst investigation notes combined with text mining techniques contribute to generating accurate labels for the machine learning models in this study?

Mining analyst investigation notes provides detailed information such as the reasons for alerts, internal and external research findings, and investigation results about whether events are malicious, false positives, or escalated. Text mining techniques, including keyword/topic extraction and sentiment analysis, are applied to these notes to extract the host's actual state by identifying relevant topics that indicate normal or risky status. This combined process enables the assignment of accurate labels reflecting the true condition of hosts, which are then used as targets for the machine learning models.

The main contribution of this paper is as follows:

An advanced machine learning system is proposed and
evaluated by real industry data from Symantec. The
system can effectively reduce the resources to analyze



risk. In addition, when compared with other machine
learning models such as Multi-layer Neural Network,
Deep Neural Network, Random Forest, Support
Vector Machine and Logistic Regression, Deep Belief
Network shows the best performance for highly
unbalanced labelled data.
The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. In Section
II, related work is summarized. Section III briefly describes
Restricted Boltzmann Machine and Deep Belief Network,
which is the core machine learning method in our research.
Section IV introduces feature engineering and label creation. In
Section V, experiment results on real industry data are
discussed. Section VI concludes the whole paper.

II. RELATED WORK
A. Conventional machine learning model
Machine learning models have been applied to detect
anomalies and intrusions [2]. In [3], nine classifiers (Bayesian
Network, Logistic Regression, Random Forest etc.) were
compared in malicious traffic detection. In [4], fuzzy clustering
was introduced to decrease the false positive rate. K-Means
clustering was used towards scalable unsupervised intrusion
detection [5]. Dynamic behavior models such as Hidden
Markov Model were adopted to detect intrusions based on host’s
user profiles built from normal usage data [6].
Li et al. presented an online Support Vector Machine (SVM)
with decision tree to classify host state based on network traffic
behavior [7]. Chand et al. combined nine other machine learning
models with SVM to obtain better performance in intrusion
detection [8]. In [9], a hybrid model combining SVM, decision
tree and Naïve Bayes was proposed. In [10], enhanced SVM
was used for network anomaly detection. In [11], Meng
compared different machine learning models including artificial
neural network, SVM and decision tree for network anomaly
intrusion detection. In [12], Silva et al. used neural network and
SVM to automatically detect hosts that disseminate web spam.
B. Deep Learning Model
More complicated models such as Deep Neural Network
(DNN) and Deep Belief Network (DBN) have been applied to
identify malicious intrusion. Min-Ju et al. adopted deep neural
network for in-vehicle network intrusion detection [13].
In [14], Deep Belief Network was applied to intrusion
detection and showed better performance than SVM. In [15],
Liu et al. applied extreme learning machine to the training
process of Deep Belief Network and improved the model
performance in network intrusion detection. Current research is
mainly based on some historical simulated or experimental data
instead of real industry data, such as NSL KDD [16]. What is
more, little research analyzes the security state of machines
based on the alert information. To the best of our knowledge,
this is the first paper which applies Deep Belief Network to
evaluate host risk on real industry data.

[Question]
How do deep learning models like Deep Belief Networks improve intrusion detection compared to conventional machine learning models, based on the comparisons and findings discussed in this section?

Deep Belief Networks (DBN) improve intrusion detection by showing better performance than Support Vector Machines (SVM) and enhancing model performance when combined with extreme learning machines in the training process. They are applied to real industry data for evaluating host risk, which is less common in conventional machine learning research that often relies on simulated or experimental data.

Abstract
Hierarchies are of fundamental interest in both stochastic optimal control and
biological control due to their facilitation of a range of desirable computational
traits in a control algorithm and the possibility that they may form a core principle
of sensorimotor and cognitive control systems. However, a theoretically justiﬁed
construction of state-space hierarchies over all spatial resolutions and their evo-
lution through a policy inference process remains elusive. Here, a formalism for
deriving such normative representations of discrete Markov decision processes is
introduced in the context of graphs. The resulting hierarchies correspond to a hier-
archical policy inference algorithm approximating a discrete gradient ﬂow between
state-space trajectory densities generated by the prior and optimal policies.
1

Interpreting the local energy potential Jπ(x) as generating a planning complexity field helps explain that FP dynamics solve the planning problem by targeting the independent sources that generate the planning complexity, analogous to how taking the divergence or Laplacian identifies independent sources generating a physical field.

[Question]
How do the functions Γ+(x, x′) and Γ−(x, x′) together ensure the conservation of total probability in the policy flow as described in this section?

The functions Γ+(x, x′) and Γ−(x, x′) measure the density gain and loss at each trajectory x due to the optimal policy flux with respect to another trajectory x′. For every Γ+(x, x′) term, there is an equal and opposite Γ−(x′, x) term. This balance ensures that the sum over all trajectories of the change in probability ˙π(x) equals zero, reflecting the Fokker-Planck equation's role as a differential continuity equation that conserves total probability.

Discussion
A preliminary study of discrete gradient ﬂow in the context of hierarchical policy inference has
been presented. Amongst all possible policy inference processes, the FP equation describes the
expected time evolution of those which take the steepest descent on the free energy objective thus
establishing a principle of optimality. Considering policy inference in trajectory space implies that
state-space structure at all scales is integrated into the policy inference process. The resulting policy
ﬂow provides a normative perspective on optimal hierarchical policy inference however it remains to
be seen whether the principles established here can be usefully embedded within practical algorithms.
The basic example provided establishes a link with hierarchical representations in human cognition
and thus a potentially fruitful research direction will be to examine whether aspects of the behavioral
and neural dynamics of natural planning and problem-solving can be explained within this framework.

Solving problem (13) and computing the optimal next policy (12) would require full knowl-
edge of the environment {p(s′|s, a), r(s, a)}.
In practice, a reinforcement learning agent
would need to estimate p and r from experience, which is prohibitively expensive in large
state-action spaces. Fortunately, we can rephrase the algorithm entirely in terms of sample
averages, thus side-stepping model estimation. We replace expectations by sample averages
using the transitions (si, ai, s′
i, ri) gathered under the current policy. The agent does not
need to visit all states—local information under the current policy suﬃces to compute the
averages. Algorithm 1 summarizes the main steps of the f-divergence constrained policy
iteration.
Function approximation.
If the dimensionality of the state space is large, one can resort
to function approximation methods. We note that relaxing the steady state constraint (7)
to a “steady features constraint”

Implementation details.
Several practical improvements make Algorithm 1 faster and
robuster. First, the temperature parameter η should be decayed with iterations in order to
guarantee convergence to a solution of the original non-penalized problem. In our exper-

can be devised. Second, for α-divergences with α ≤1, one can omit the dual variables

were not sampled under the current policy can be set to the baseline λ⋆, which serves as
a proxy for the expected reward; in our experiments this strategy performed best. Fourth,
introducing a little slack in the linear constraint on the domain of the convex conjugate
function improves robustness and helps the optimizer.

[Question]
How does the use of sample averages in the f-divergence constrained policy iteration algorithm enable reinforcement learning agents to avoid the prohibitive expense of environment model estimation in large state-action spaces?

The use of sample averages in the f-divergence constrained policy iteration algorithm allows reinforcement learning agents to replace expectations with averages computed from transitions gathered under the current policy. This approach relies only on local information from the current policy's experience, eliminating the need to estimate the full environment model {p(s′|s, a), r(s, a)}, which is prohibitively expensive in large state-action spaces.

Abstract
To ensure stability of learning, state-of-the-art generalized policy iteration algorithms aug-
ment the policy improvement step with a trust region constraint bounding the information
loss. The size of the trust region is commonly determined by the Kullback-Leibler (KL)
divergence, which not only captures the notion of distance well but also yields closed-form
solutions. In this paper, we consider a more general class of f-divergences and derive the
corresponding policy update rules. The generic solution is expressed through the derivative
of the convex conjugate function to f and includes the KL solution as a special case. Within
the class of f-divergences, we further focus on a one-parameter family of α-divergences to
study eﬀects of the choice of divergence on policy improvement. Previously known as well
as new policy updates emerge for diﬀerent values of α. We show that every type of policy
update comes with a compatible policy evaluation resulting from the chosen f-divergence.
Interestingly, the mean-squared Bellman error minimization is closely related to policy
evaluation with the Pearson χ2-divergence penalty, while the KL divergence results in the
soft-max policy update and a log-sum-exp critic.
We carry out asymptotic analysis of
the solutions for diﬀerent values of α and demonstrate the eﬀects of using diﬀerent diver-
gence functions on a multi-armed bandit problem and on common standard reinforcement
learning problems.
Keywords:
Reinforcement Learning, Policy Search, Bandit Problems

[Question]
How does the choice of different f-divergences, particularly within the α-divergence family, influence the policy improvement and evaluation steps in reinforcement learning as discussed in this paper?

The choice of different f-divergences, especially within the α-divergence family, influences policy improvement by yielding different policy update rules, including previously known and new updates for various α values. Each type of policy update corresponds to a compatible policy evaluation derived from the chosen f-divergence. For example, policy evaluation with the Pearson χ2-divergence penalty relates closely to mean-squared Bellman error minimization, while the KL divergence leads to a soft-max policy update and a log-sum-exp critic. The paper also presents asymptotic analysis showing how different divergence functions affect learning outcomes in reinforcement learning tasks.

5. Conclusion
In this paper, we have developed a framework for deriving new and established policy
improvement and policy iteration algorithms by constraining policy change using the f-
divergence. First, we described the key idea in the bandit scenario. Looking at the smooth
curve of α-divergences in the space of f-divergences, we were able to see the connection
between many classical action selection strategies as well as discover several new ones.
The optimal choice of α depends on the time horizon, since diﬀerent α’s corresponds to
diﬀerent exploration strategies. The soft-max policy update rule, employed by the Exp3
15
Belousov and Peters
algorithm (Bubeck and Cesa-Bianchi, 2012) and the gradient bandit algorithm (Sutton
and Barto, 1998), is a special case of the f-divergence constrained policy improvement
corresponding to the KL divergence.
After the bandit case, we considered the average-reward reinforcement learning problem,
for which we found both (i) an eﬃcient sample-based solution and (ii) the important new
insight that the choice of the f-divergence does not only imply the type of policy improve-
ment step but also the compatible policy evaluation step. In particular, we have shown that
employing the soft-max policy for the actor implies that the log-sum-exp function is used for
policy evaluation by the critic (somewhat similar to REPS (Peters et al., 2010)). Similarly,
in our framework, policy evaluation by minimizing the mean squared diﬀerential advantage,
which is closely related to the mean squared Bellman error, results from the choice of the
α = 2-divergence and comes along with a new, compatible policy update rule. Thus, it is
likely that this approach will still yield a larger number of new policy improvements along
with their compatible critics derived by just choosing another f-divergence.

[Question]
How do the policy evaluation and improvement steps differ between the KL divergence (α = 1) and Pearson χ2 divergence (α = 2) cases, and what implications do these differences have for the roles of the baseline λ⋆ and the dual variables κ?

For the KL divergence (α = 1) case, the policy evaluation step involves minimizing the Lagrangian dual function g(v), where the baseline λ⋆ equals this dual function and depends on v. The policy improvement step uses a soft-max update: the new policy π⋆(a|s) is proportional to the old state-action distribution q(s,a) multiplied by the exponential of the advantage scaled by η. The baseline λ⋆ cancels out in the policy but appears in the state-action distribution.

In contrast, for the Pearson χ2 divergence (α = 2), the baseline λ⋆ becomes the average return J(π0), independent of v. The dual variables κ are important to ensure non-negativity of probabilities and may not be zero unless the temperature η exceeds the absolute minimum differential advantage. The policy evaluation step minimizes the squared differential advantage scaled by 2η, and the policy improvement step corresponds to a linear reweighting of the old policy based on the differential advantage.

These differences imply that in the KL divergence case, λ⋆ is a function of v and central to the evaluation step, while κ are zero. In the Pearson χ2 case, λ⋆ is fixed and independent of v, and κ may be nonzero to enforce constraints, affecting the feasibility of the policy. Thus, the choice of divergence affects both the form of policy evaluation and improvement and the roles of λ⋆ and κ in the optimization.

In this paper, we have developed a framework for deriving new and established policy
improvement and policy iteration algorithms by constraining policy change using the f-

[Question]
How does the choice of f-divergence influence both the policy improvement and policy evaluation steps in the reinforcement learning framework presented in this paper, and why is this compatibility significant?

The choice of f-divergence determines both the type of policy improvement step and the compatible policy evaluation step in the reinforcement learning framework. For example, using the soft-max policy (corresponding to the KL divergence) leads to policy evaluation via the log-sum-exp function, while choosing the α = 2-divergence results in policy evaluation by minimizing the mean squared differential advantage, which is linked to the mean squared Bellman error, along with a new compatible policy update rule. This compatibility is significant because it ensures that the policy improvement and evaluation steps are aligned, enabling efficient and coherent learning processes and allowing the derivation of new policy improvements and compatible critics by selecting different f-divergences.

[Question]
How does the parameter averaging approach proposed in this section address the challenges of adapting neural network acoustic models, and why is it considered effective compared to updating all model parameters?

The parameter averaging approach addresses the challenge of adapting neural network acoustic models, which have a huge number of parameters making full adaptation difficult, by updating only a small part of the model and then averaging the parameters of the original seed model and its adapted version. This method is effective because it leverages the concept used in Kaldi’s NNET3 training, where models updated separately on different data subsets are averaged to stabilize and improve the model. By averaging parameters instead of updating all parameters directly, it reduces the risk of overfitting and instability during adaptation, leading to better performance as shown by improved word error rates after parameter averaging.

[Question]
How does the two-compartment model of pyramidal neurons facilitate the comparison between desired target and actual prediction in the dendritic predictive plasticity framework, and why is the apical compartment omitted for output neurons?

The two-compartment model of pyramidal neurons facilitates the comparison between desired target and actual prediction by explicitly incorporating basal and apical dendritic integration zones, allowing the neuron to compare these signals. In the dendritic predictive plasticity framework, two compartments suffice for this comparison. The apical compartment is omitted for output neurons (gA = 0) to simplify the model, and basal voltages are used for prediction. Output neurons receive direct external teaching input via synapses proximal to the soma, providing the desired target signal as an additional somatic current.

[Question]
Why is it important for the correlation matrix E[Q] to be positive definite in the learning dynamics of lateral weights, and how do the properties of the neurons and initial weight settings influence this condition?

The correlation matrix E[Q] must be positive definite to ensure that the synaptic dynamics align within 90° of the gradient, leading to the unique minimum of the loss function LIP_k. For linear neurons, this condition requires that the input patterns span the entire space RN_k, which is typically met when uncorrelated background noise currents are present or in deterministic networks solving nontrivial tasks. For nonlinear neurons with a monotonically increasing transfer function φ, saturation can make the matrix nearly singular and slow down learning. Therefore, initializing the weight matrix so that neurons operate far from saturation helps maintain the positive definiteness of E[Q] and supports effective learning.

How does the incorporation of dendritic predictive plasticity and the additional top-down weights enable the network to implement an approximate inverse of the forward model, and what dual roles do these connections play in improving recognition performance?

The incorporation of dendritic predictive plasticity shapes the top-down weights from output to hidden neurons and from hidden neurons back to the input area, enabling these top-down synapses to learn to predict the activities of the corresponding lower area. This learning implements an approximate inverse of the forward model. These connections play a dual role by (1) communicating back upper area activities to improve the hidden neuron representation on a recognition task, and (2) learning to invert the induced forward model. This paired encoder-decoder architecture enhances recognition performance by providing useful error signals to hidden areas and improving reconstructions of hidden neuron activities and input images.

We have also shown that top-down synapses can be learned using the same dendritic predictive
learning rule used at the remaining connections. In our model, the top-down connections have a
dual role: they are involved in the apical error representation and, they learn to match the somatic
ﬁring driven by the bottom-up input (Urbanczik and Senn, 2014). The simultaneous learning of the
bottom-up and top-down pathways leads to the formation of a generative network that can denoise
sensory input or generate dream-like inputs (Fig. 5).
Finally, the framework introduced here could also be adapted to other types of error-based learn-
ing, such as in generative models that instead of learning to discriminate sensory inputs, learn to
generate following sensory input statistics. Error propagation in these forms of generative models,
which arise from an inaccurate prediction of sensory inputs, may rely on different dendritic com-
partments and interneurons, such as the previously mentioned PV inhibitory cells (Petreanu et al.,

the brain, there can be a substantial delay between the lateral excitatory input and the feedback
from other brain areas (in the order of tens to hundreds of milliseconds (Cauller and Kulics, 1991;
Larkum, 2013)), suggesting that the lateral inhibitory interaction mediated by SST cells should be
also delayed and tuned to the feedback.
Interestingly, there is strong experimental support for
a delayed inhibition mediated by pyramidal-to-SST connections (Silberberg and Markram, 2007;
Murayama et al., 2009; Berger et al., 2009; Berger et al., 2010), which could in principle be tuned
to match both the delay and magnitude of the feedback signal. Moreover, the spontaneous ac-

by neighbouring pyramidal cells. We would predict that these levels of spontaneous ﬁring rates in
SST should match the level of feedback received by the pyramidal cells targeted by a particular
SST interneuron. In addition, our model predicts the need for a weak top-down input onto SST in-

indeed provide such a precise cancellation of the top-down excitatory inputs (Zhang et al., 2014;
Leinweber et al., 2017).

a high-level prediction error occurring at some higher cortical area would imply also lower-level
prediction errors to co-occur at earlier areas. For instance, a categorization error occurring when a
visual object is misclassiﬁed, would be signalled backwards through our interneuron circuits to lower
areas where the individual visual features of the objects are represented. Recent experimental ob-
servations in the macaque face-processing hierarchy support this view (Schwiedrzik and Freiwald,
2017). We predict that higher-area activity modulates lower-area activity with the purpose to shape
synaptic plasticity at these lower areas.
Here we have focused on the role of SST cells as a feedback-speciﬁc interneuron. There are

Conclusion
In this paper, based on the structure of SQL and the observation that a sequence-
to-sequence model suﬀer from the ”order-matters” problem, we design speciﬁc
deep neural network for each sub-string of SQL. In the WHERE prediction mod-
ule, we choose the top probabilities of the column candidates as the chosen set
for the prediction of conditions. We apply the bi-directional attention mechanism
and the CNN-based character-level embedding to improve the result. The ex-
perimental evaluations show that our model achieves the state-of-the-art results
in the WikiSQL dataset.
We observe that the accuracy is around 90% on the COLUMN-SELECT
clause prediction and AGGREGATOR-SELECT clause prediction because the
number of candidate column in the SELECT clause is limited to one. The task
will be more challenging if the SQL extends to more than one column candi-
dates and more complex cases like ORDER-BY, GROUP-BY or even JOIN.
And the technique of NL2SQL can be applied to Knowledge Graph query or
other semantic parsing tasks. There will be a lot of work to research.

[Question]
How does the fixed SQL structure described in the WikiSQL task (Chunk A) influence the design choices, such as the bi-directional attention mechanism and the handling of column candidates in the WHERE prediction module, leading to the state-of-the-art results reported in the paper (Chunk B)?

The fixed SQL structure in the WikiSQL task, which consists of a SELECT clause with one column and optional aggregator, and a WHERE clause with 0-4 conditions without JOINs, directly influences the model design. Because the SQL structure is fixed and limited, the model can be specialized for each SQL sub-string, avoiding the "order-matters" problem typical in sequence-to-sequence models. This specialization allows the WHERE prediction module to select top probabilities from column candidates effectively as the chosen set for condition prediction. Additionally, the bi-directional attention mechanism and CNN-based character-level embedding are applied to enhance understanding of the question and table columns within this fixed structure. These design choices leverage the constrained SQL format to improve prediction accuracy, achieving state-of-the-art results on the WikiSQL dataset.

[Question]
How does the basis-kernel representation explain the limitation on the number of Householder transformations needed in a Householder flow?

The basis-kernel representation shows that any orthogonal matrix Q can be expressed as Q = I − YSY⊤, where Y is a full-rank matrix defining the basis and S is a nonsingular kernel matrix. The degree of Q, defined as the dimension of its active subspace R(Y), is bounded by the size k of Y. Since a Householder matrix has degree 1 and the degree of an orthogonal matrix is bounded by k, the number of Householder transformations needed in a Householder flow is also bounded by k. Thus, the basis-kernel representation limits the number of Householder transformations by bounding the degree of the orthogonal matrix.

Conclusions
We have proposed S2VGD, an eﬃcient Bayesian pos-
terior learning scheme for the weights of BNNs with
structural MVG priors.
To achieve this, we derive
a new reparametrization for the MVG to unify pre-
vious structural priors, and adopt the SVGD algo-
rithm for accurate posterior learning. By transform-
ing the MVG into a lower-dimensional representation,
S2VGD avoids computation of related kernel matri-
ces in high-dimensional space.
The eﬀectiveness of
our framework is validated on several real-world tasks,
including regression, classiﬁcation, contextual bandits
and reinforcement learning.
Extensive experimental
results demonstrate its superiority relative to related
algorithms.
Our empirical results on sequential decision-making
suggest the beneﬁts of including inter-weight struc-
ture within the model, when computing policy uncer-
tainty for online decision-making in an uncertain envi-
ronments. More sophisticated methods for leveraging
uncertainty for exploration/exploration balance may
be a promising direction for future work. For exam-
ple, explicitly encouraging exploration using learned
structural uncertainty [Houthooft et al., 2016].

4. DISCUSSION AND CONCLUSIONS
Traditionally, patient data cleaning has accounted for approximately 80% of the time spent on data
analysis in clinical research projects. In this work, we reported an initial effort towards automated patient
data cleaning, specifically, the task of standardizing organ labels. Assigning standardized labels to the
organs is crucial to avoiding the misinterpretation of critical details, which can result in treatment errors
and radiation therapy misadministration. We have shown that deep learning can achieve accurate organ
identification and standardized labeling without manual intervention, thus eliminating errors and
inconsistencies in labeling patient data. We tested the model on patients with head and neck or prostate
cancer. In total, 154 patients’ data (100 prostate cases and 54 head and neck cases) were utilized in this
study. Our model identified organ contours with an accuracy of 100% and assigned standardized labels.

patient with head and neck cancer.

In our future work on patient data cleaning, we focus on two areas: increasing the number of organs
labeled for head and neck cases and contouring errors. We intend to incorporate a comprehensive list of
head and neck organs (this was not possible in this study due to the small patient data set that we had to
work with) as prescribed by our institution. Our goal is to develop a 3D model that accepts volumetric
anatomical data to identify organs automatically. This will allow the model to learn high-dimensional
volumetric features. We intend to add this model to our current clinical system to automate the procedure
of standardizing organ labels and retrospectively applying the model to existing patient data to promote
clinical research, especially multi-institutional collaborative clinical research.
We are also working to identify and correct contour errors using deep learning. Errors such as incorrect
contouring, contour-label mismatch, and volume fill (not closing the manually contoured volume), among
others, could result in treatment errors and significantly lengthen the treatment planning process. Currently,
at our institution, it takes 7-10 days to plan treatment for head and neck cases after the CT simulation. If
contouring errors are detected, the required rework would increase treatment planning time between 28%
to 57%. In addition, this also adds logistic complexity to the whole process, because it involves many
people, including the physician, dosimetrist, physicists, and the Quality Assurance (QA) team, hence the
need for automated tools.

2.1 Organ Labeling Problem
Organ labeling in radiation therapy is the process where the physician manually contours critical organs
near the treatment site and assigns labels to them. In many institutions, there are currently no automated
processes to assist physicians in this task. Our novel approach addresses the issue of organ labeling. We
present a two-step process of assigning standardized labels to the organs identified: Step 1. Automatically
identify the organs contoured for a specific treatment site, and Step 2. Assign standardized labels in
accordance with Mayo et al’s standardized organ nomenclature (Mayo et al 2015).
In the first step, we acquire organ contour points along with their labels from the RT Structure DICOM

How does the design of the CNN model's convolutional and subsampling layers, including the doubling of kernel numbers and the use of max pooling, contribute to the extraction and retention of important features for organ identification in the composite images?

The CNN model's design uses three convolutional layers where the number of kernels doubles at each layer (64, 128, 256), enabling the extraction of increasingly complex and high-dimensional localized features from the composite images. [Question]
How does the design of the CNN architecture, including the doubling of kernel numbers and the use of max pooling, contribute to the effective extraction and reduction of image features for organ identification in this study?

The CNN architecture uses three convolutional layers with kernel numbers doubled at each layer (64, 128, 256), enabling the extraction of increasingly complex and high-dimensional localized features. Max pooling with 2 × 2 contiguous windows is applied after each convolutional layer, reducing the size of feature maps by half each time (e.g., from 128 × 64 to 64 × 32), which retains the most prominent localized features while reducing spatial dimensions. This combination allows the CNN to effectively learn key image-contrast features and reduce image complexity for accurate organ identification.

[Question]
How do the differences in weight cluster patterns and variance explained by the top PCA modes for learning rates 10𝑒−3 and 10𝑒−1 reflect the CNN model's ability to learn meaningful features and achieve accurate organ classification?

For learning rate 10𝑒−3, the CNN model showed two weight clusters after the convolutional step and a single cluster after the fully-connected step, indicating extraction of key localized features and accurate organ classification. The top three PCA modes explained 4% variance initially, 98% after convolution, and 99.9% after fully-connected optimization, reflecting learning of high-dimensional meaningful features. In contrast, for learning rate 10𝑒−1, many weight clusters appeared after the convolutional step, indicating conflicting features, and clusters persisted after the fully-connected step. This corresponded with poor convergence and about 10% accuracy, showing the model failed to learn meaningful features. Thus, fewer, well-defined weight clusters and higher variance explained by PCA modes at 10𝑒−3 reflect better feature learning and accurate classification, while multiple conflicting clusters and poor variance patterns at 10𝑒−1 reflect poor learning and low accuracy.

How does the choice of learning rate ηi in the Mirror Descent sub-algorithms ensure the meta-algorithm achieves the stated regret bound, and what role does the strong convexity parameter λ play in this guarantee?

The choice of learning rate ηi = RiL√(λ/n) in each Mirror Descent sub-algorithm ensures that the regret for sub-algorithm Algi satisfies

∑_{t=1}^n ft(wi_t) − inf_{w∈Wi} ∑_{t=1}^n ft(w) ≤ O(RiL√(n/λ)).

This is because, by Fact 1, when the regularizer R is λ-strongly convex, the Mirror Descent iterates satisfy a regret bound involving η, λ, and the gradients. Setting ηi proportional to RiL√(λ/n) balances the terms in the regret bound, controlling the trade-off between the learning rate and the strong convexity.

The strong convexity parameter λ plays a crucial role by appearing in the denominator inside the square root in the regret bound, effectively scaling the regret. A larger λ (stronger convexity) leads to a smaller regret bound, improving the guarantee. Thus, λ ensures that the Mirror Descent updates have sufficient curvature to control the regret growth, enabling the meta-algorithm to achieve the stated overall regret bound.

[Question]
How does the admissibility condition ensure that the randomized strategy from Algorithm 3 maintains the relaxation Rel as an upper bound over time, and why is the use of Jensen's inequality important in this reasoning?

The admissibility condition ensures that at each timestep t, the expected value of the learner's loss plus the relaxation Rel at time t does not exceed the relaxation Rel at time t−1. Specifically, it requires showing that

inf_{Pt ∈ Δ(ΔN)} sup_{gt ∈ C} E_{pt ∼ Pt} E_{it ∼ pt}[⟨eit, gt⟩ + Rel(g₁:ₜ)] ≤ Rel(g₁:ₜ₋₁).

By plugging in the randomized strategy from Algorithm 3, the expectation over the randomization σ_{t+1:n} replaces the distribution Pt, and the inequality is verified by bounding the combined expected loss and relaxation term.

Jensen's inequality is important because it allows exchanging the order of the supremum and expectation, enabling the grouping of expectations and bounding the supremum of a sum by the sum of suprema in expectation. This step is crucial to handle the randomness introduced by the strategy and to maintain the relaxation Rel as an upper bound over time.

[Question]
Why is the simple approach of aggregating online learning algorithms for each subclass using multiplicative weights insufficient for online model selection, and how does the multi-scale algorithm proposed in the paper address this challenge?

The simple approach of aggregating online learning algorithms for each subclass using multiplicative weights is insufficient because the losses of predictors or experts for each subclass may grow with the subclass index, causing the regret to scale with the largest subclass rather than the target subclass. The multi-scale algorithm addresses this by adapting the regret to the range of each expert's losses, allowing it to handle experts with losses in different ranges and incorporate a prior reflecting their importance, thus overcoming the scaling issue.

We now focus on the trickier “not dominated” case.
Rescaling doesn’t induce domination
We ﬁrst observe that if (w,c) does is not dominated, (w,Bc)
is not dominated either for any B ≥1. Let j be the index for which wj + cj ≥w−j + c−j which implies
wj −cj ≤w−j + c−j because (w,c) is not dominated. Observe that if (w,Bc) is dominated we either have
wj −Bcj ≥w−j +Bc−j or w−j −Bc−j ≥wj +Bcj. The ﬁrst case cannot hold because B ≥1 and we already know
that (w,c) is not dominated. The second case in particular implies w−j ≥wj, so we must have had cj ≥c−j
to begin with. But in that case we will still have wj + Bcj ≥w−j + Bc−j which contradicts the domination.
Note: It is good to keep in mind that while rescaling does not induce domination, it may not be the case
in general that wj +Bcj ≥w−j +Bc−j even though wj +cj ≥w−j +c−j. That is, the “leader” may change after
rescaling.
